responses:
Hey! 👋 I’m TasteBot, your cooking buddy. I can help you figure out what to cook and guide you step by step so nothing feels confusing, even if you're just starting out.

You can tell me a dish you want or just list your ingredients. I’ll break everything down into simple steps you can follow easily. So, what are we cooking today?

Adobo is one of the easiest Filipino dishes you can start with, and it works even if you’re not an experienced cook. You’ll need chicken or pork, soy sauce, vinegar, garlic, bay leaves, and peppercorns. The flavor comes from balancing salty, sour, and savory tastes.

Step by step: (1) Marinate your meat in soy sauce and garlic for 20–30 minutes so it absorbs flavor. (2) Put everything in a pot, add vinegar, bay leaves, and peppercorns, then bring it to a boil without stirring too early. (3) Lower the heat and simmer for 30–40 minutes until the meat becomes tender. (4) Let the sauce reduce until it becomes rich and slightly thick.

If you want it better, you can fry the meat first or add a little sugar for balance. Do you want a spicy version or the classic one?

Sinigang is a warm sour soup that feels comforting, especially on rainy days. It’s made with pork, shrimp, or fish, and vegetables like radish, eggplant, and kangkong. The sour taste usually comes from tamarind or sinigang mix.

Here’s how to cook it: (1) Boil your meat with onions and tomatoes until it becomes tender. (2) Add your souring agent and adjust the taste depending on how sour you like it. (3) Add vegetables, starting with the hard ones like radish and eggplant. (4) Simmer for a few more minutes until everything is cooked.

You can add fish sauce for more flavor. Do you like it very sour or just mild?

Lumpia is crispy, tasty, and fun to make once you get used to wrapping it. It usually has ground pork, carrots, onion, garlic, and a thin wrapper.

Step by step: (1) Mix all filling ingredients until well combined. (2) Put a small amount on the wrapper and roll it tightly, sealing the edge with water. (3) Heat oil and fry until golden brown and crispy. (4) Drain excess oil before serving.

Don’t overcrowd the pan so they cook evenly. Do you want tips on how to wrap it faster without breaking?

Tinola is a simple chicken soup that’s light but very comforting. It uses ginger, garlic, onion, chicken, and vegetables like papaya or chayote.

Here’s how to cook it: (1) Sauté garlic, onion, and ginger until fragrant. (2) Add chicken and cook until it changes color. (3) Pour water and simmer until the chicken becomes tender. (4) Add papaya or chayote and cook until soft. (5) Add chili leaves last.

Season with fish sauce for better flavor. Do you prefer strong ginger taste or mild soup?

Pancit Canton is a quick noodle dish you can easily customize depending on what you have at home. It usually includes noodles, meat, vegetables, and soy sauce.

Step by step: (1) Sauté garlic and meat until cooked. (2) Add vegetables and cook lightly so they stay crunchy. (3) Pour broth or water with soy sauce. (4) Add noodles and mix until they absorb flavor. (5) Stir everything together evenly.

Don’t overcook the noodles so they stay firm. Do you want me to adjust this based on your ingredients?

Turon is a sweet crispy snack made with banana and sugar wrapped in spring roll wrappers. It’s simple but very satisfying when fried properly.

Here’s how: (1) Slice banana and coat it with sugar. (2) Wrap it tightly in the wrapper. (3) Fry in medium heat until golden brown. (4) Let the sugar caramelize while frying.

You can add jackfruit for extra flavor. Do you want it extra crispy or just lightly fried?

If you're just starting, fried rice is one of the best dishes to learn basic cooking skills. It teaches you how to sauté, season, and mix ingredients properly.

Step by step: (1) Heat oil and sauté garlic until fragrant. (2) Add egg or meat and cook it. (3) Add rice and break clumps. (4) Season with soy sauce or salt. (5) Mix and taste.

Keep it simple first—don’t rush. Do you want me to suggest something based on what you have in your kitchen?

Fried rice or sinangag is one of the most common Filipino breakfasts 🍳. It’s simple, fast, and perfect for leftover rice.

To cook it, (1) heat oil in a pan and sauté garlic until golden and fragrant. (2) Add egg or meat like hotdog or leftover chicken. (3) Add cold rice and break it apart while mixing. (4) Season with soy sauce or salt and stir well until evenly coated.

It’s best when rice is a bit dry so it doesn’t get soggy. Do you want garlic fried rice or egg fried rice version?

Tocino is a sweet Filipino cured pork often eaten for breakfast with rice and egg 🍚.

To cook it, (1) pan-fry the tocino without oil first so it releases its natural glaze. (2) Add a little water so it cooks evenly. (3) Let it simmer until the sauce thickens and caramelizes. (4) Fry until slightly crispy edges form.

Serve it with rice and egg for a classic Pinoy breakfast. Do you want homemade tocino or store-bought style cooking guide?

Longganisa is a Filipino sausage that can be sweet, garlicky, or spicy depending on the region 🌭.

To cook it, (1) place longganisa in a pan with a little water. (2) Let it boil until water evaporates. (3) Once oil comes out, fry it in its own fat until golden brown. (4) Serve with garlic rice and egg.

Do you want Vigan (garlicky) or Lucban (sour-ish) style?

Daing na bangus is a marinated milkfish that’s crispy and tangy when fried 🐟.

To cook it, (1) marinate bangus in vinegar, garlic, salt, and pepper overnight. (2) Heat oil and fry skin-side first until crispy. (3) Flip and cook the other side until golden. (4) Drain excess oil before serving.

Best eaten with rice and spiced vinegar. Do you like it crispy or soft fried?

Bulalo is a beef bone marrow soup popular in Batangas 🥣. It’s rich, comforting, and perfect for cold weather.

To cook it, (1) boil beef shank and bones for 1–2 hours until tender. (2) Skim off impurities. (3) Add corn and potatoes. (4) Add cabbage and pechay last. (5) Season with salt or fish sauce.

The soup becomes flavorful from the bone marrow. Do you want a budget version or restaurant-style?

Chicken Inasal is a grilled chicken dish from Bacolod with a smoky and tangy flavor 🔥.

To cook it, (1) marinate chicken in calamansi, vinegar, garlic, ginger, and annatto oil. (2) Grill while basting with marinade and oil. (3) Flip until fully cooked and slightly charred. (4) Serve with rice and dipping sauce.

Do you want charcoal grill version or pan-grilled style?

Sisig is a sizzling Filipino dish made from chopped pork, onions, and calamansi 🔥.

To cook it, (1) boil pork until tender. (2) Grill or fry for texture. (3) Chop finely. (4) Sauté with onions, chili, and calamansi juice. (5) Serve sizzling with egg on top.

Do you want crispy sisig or creamy mayo-style sisig?

Giniling is a simple Filipino ground pork dish often cooked at home 🍲.

To make it, (1) sauté garlic and onion. (2) Add ground pork and cook until browned. (3) Add carrots, potatoes, and peas. (4) Pour tomato sauce and simmer until tender.

It’s perfect with rice and very budget-friendly. Do you want spicy giniling or classic version?

Arroz caldo is a Filipino rice porridge with chicken and ginger 🍲.

To cook it, (1) sauté garlic, onion, and ginger. (2) Add chicken and cook slightly. (3) Add rice and broth. (4) Stir until rice becomes soft and porridge-like. (5) Top with egg and fried garlic.

It’s best eaten warm. Do you want it thick or soupy?

Lugaw is a simple Filipino rice porridge often eaten when you’re sick or want something light 🍚.

To cook it, (1) wash rice and boil with water. (2) Stir often so it doesn’t stick. (3) Add salt or ginger for flavor. (4) Top with egg or chicken.

It’s very easy and comforting. Do you want plain lugaw or with chicken?

Banana cue is a popular street snack made with fried saba bananas 🍌.

To cook it, (1) fry saba bananas in hot oil. (2) Add brown sugar while frying so it caramelizes. (3) Cook until golden and sticky. (4) Skewer and serve hot.

Do you want extra caramelized or lightly sweet?

Kwek-kwek is a popular Filipino street food made from quail eggs covered in orange batter 🟠.

To cook it, (1) boil quail eggs and peel them. (2) Dip in orange batter made of flour and food coloring. (3) Deep fry until crispy. (4) Serve with vinegar or sweet sauce.

Do you want street-style sauce recipe too?

Fishballs are a famous Filipino street food usually served on skewers 🍢.

To cook it, (1) deep fry fishballs until golden. (2) Prepare sauce using soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and cornstarch. (3) Simmer until thick. (4) Serve hot with skewers.

Do you want Manong fishball sauce style?

Siomai is a steamed dumpling filled with pork, shrimp, or beef 🥟.

To cook it, (1) prepare ground meat mixture with seasonings. (2) wrap in siomai wrappers. (3) steam for 10–15 minutes. (4) serve with soy sauce, calamansi, and chili oil.

Do you want street-style siomai sauce?

Buko pie is a famous Filipino coconut pie from Laguna 🥧.

To make it, (1) prepare pie crust. (2) cook young coconut with milk and sugar. (3) fill crust and cover with top layer. (4) bake until golden brown.

Do you want easy no-bake version?

Puto bumbong is a purple rice cake usually eaten during Christmas 🎄.

To make it, (1) steam purple glutinous rice in bamboo tubes. (2) Serve with butter, sugar, and coconut. (3) Eat while hot.
